questions.question: Surface fatigue cracks are suspected on an in-service part. Alternating current is the classic choice for this inspection because:
options: AC penetrates deeper than any DC waveform, reaching cracks well below the surface
the skin effect concentrates the field at the surface where fatigue cracks start, and the alternating field vibrates particles toward leakage fields
AC leaves a strong residual field, so particles can be applied long after the shot
AC eliminates arc-strike risk at the contact points
questions.explanation: AC rides the skin of the part, concentrating flux exactly where service-induced fatigue cracks begin, and its continuous reversals keep particles dancing so they migrate to leakage fields. The same skin effect is the method's limit: AC finds nothing deep.

questions.question: Full-wave DC is selected for a deep-penetrating bench inspection of a hardened part. The trade-off that follows the part to the demagnetization station is:
options: FWDC fields cannot be removed by any demagnetization method
FWDC leaves no residual field, so the demagnetization step is skipped
the deep FWDC residual field is the hardest to remove, and AC demagnetizing equipment cannot reach it
FWDC magnetizes only the part surface, leaving the core field-free
questions.explanation: FWDC penetrates deepest, so its residual field lives deep in the section. Because AC demagnetizers are surface-bound by the skin effect, a deep FWDC residual survives every AC pass — removing it takes reversing, step-down DC.

questions.question: Worried about missing fine cracks, a technician runs a head shot well above the procedure's amperage range. The most likely result is:
options: a furry, feathery particle background with pileup at corners and section changes that can mask real indications
higher sensitivity to fine cracks with no practical downside
no visible change — excess current only lengthens the demagnetization step
particles stop responding because the field exceeds their magnetic saturation
questions.explanation: Too much flux makes the surface 'fur' with feathery nonrelevant background and piles particles at sharp corners and section changes, drowning real indications in noise. Flux density is right when a QQI shows clearly against a quiet background — too little hides tight cracks, but too much hides them just as effectively.

questions.question: The continuous method — particles applied while the magnetizing current is on — is the default sequence in MT because:
options: it is the only sequence that works on high-retentivity alloys
it allows both field directions to be energized and inspected in a single shot
it eliminates the need to prove flux density with a QQI
the surface field is at its maximum while the particles are still mobile, giving the highest sensitivity
questions.explanation: Sensitivity peaks when particles arrive while the field is at full strength — flood, divert, then shoot while the film can still move. The residual method (magnetize first, bathe after) is the exception: permitted only on high-retentivity parts and only when the procedure explicitly allows it.

questions.question: A 4340 shaft is inspected in the annealed condition, and an identical shaft after quench-and-tempering. Compared with the annealed shaft, the hardened one:
options: behaves identically, because alloy chemistry alone fixes magnetic properties
holds a stronger residual field and resists demagnetization, because the microstructure that pins dislocations also pins domain walls
is magnetically softer, accepting and releasing flux more easily
is no longer ferromagnetic until it is stress-relieved
questions.explanation: Heat treatment changes a steel's magnetic personality. Annealed, the bar is magnetically soft — high permeability, low retentivity — and must be inspected by the continuous method. Hardened, retentivity and coercive force both rise: the part may qualify for the residual method and will need real demagnetization afterward.

questions.question: Which list contains only genuine consequences of leaving a residual field in a part?
options: accelerated corrosion, paint adhesion failure, hydrogen embrittlement
reduced tensile strength, surface decarburization, thread galling
arc blow in later welding, chips clinging during machining, wear debris drawn into bearings, disturbed compasses and instruments
increased hardness, shortened fatigue life, loss of fracture toughness
questions.explanation: Residual magnetism is a magnetic nuisance, not a metallurgical defect: it bends welding arcs (arc blow), holds chips and grit that score finishes and dull tools, drags fine wear debris into bearings, and disturbs compasses and instruments — the classic reasons procedures order demagnetization.

questions.question: After inspection is complete, critical procedures often require a part magnetized only by head shots to be magnetized longitudinally before the final demagnetization. The purpose is to:
options: convert the contained circular residual into an external, measurable field so the verification reading after demagnetization means something
strengthen the circular field for one last inspection pass
cancel the circular field directly, since opposing fields annihilate each other
complete the two-direction coverage requirement for flaw detection
questions.explanation: A circular residual field closes its flux loop entirely inside the part: no external poles, a near-zero meter reading, and full strength all the same. Magnetizing longitudinally first converts the hidden field into one with external poles that a field indicator or gaussmeter can actually verify after demag.

questions.question: Demagnetizing with an AC pass-through coil is done correctly by:
options: holding the part stationary in the coil for one minute, then switching the current off
withdrawing the part as fast as possible so the field collapses before it can re-magnetize the part
running baskets of small parts through together to keep production moving
feeding the part along the coil axis and withdrawing it slowly to about 3–4 feet before current is removed, traveling roughly east–west
questions.explanation: The line current supplies the reversals; slow withdrawal supplies the diminishing taper, which must continue to about 3–4 feet before the current is removed. East–west travel keeps the earth's field from realigning the part as it exits, and parts go through one at a time — pieces piled in a basket shield each other.

questions.question: A ring gear's bore is fully covered with overlapping offset-central-conductor shots. Which expected flaw orientation does this plan still miss, and what is the fix?
options: axial cracks at the tooth roots — add more shots around the bore
subsurface flaws of every orientation — switch to dry powder
circumferential cracks, which lie parallel to the circular flux — add a coil shot or induced-current magnetization where the procedure requires it
bore-surface cracks — a central conductor magnetizes only the outer diameter
questions.explanation: Flux only leaks where it crosses a flaw at roughly 45–90°. The conductor's circular field runs along the circumference — perpendicular to axial cracks (found) but parallel to circumferential cracks (missed). Where that orientation matters, the plan adds a roughly perpendicular magnetization such as a coil shot or induced current.

questions.question: In the offset central conductor technique, QQI shims are placed at the edges of each coverage band because:
options: the field weakens with distance from the conductor, making the band edge the worst case that must be proven
the field is strongest at the band edges, where false background is most likely
the center of each band receives no field, so a shim there would never show
QQIs respond only to longitudinal fields, which exist only at the band edges
questions.explanation: With the bar resting against the bore wall, each shot reliably covers only about four conductor diameters of circumference, and the field falls off with distance from the bar. Proving the field at the band edge — the worst case — proves the whole band, which is why the gear is rotated through overlapping shots with QQIs at the edges.
